subsystem: SDDM (Simple Desktop Display Manager) PAM authentication backend. This is the PamBackend::start() method of the sddm-helper auth process, which selects a PAM service name (an /etc/pam.d policy) and hands it, together with the requested username, to the wrapped PAM object (m_pam) to begin an authentication conversation. Its job is to choose which authentication policy governs this login attempt (interactive login, greeter, password check, or autologin) and to report failure to start the PAM stack.
provenance: Identified by the SDDM-specific PAM service strings "sddm", "sddm-greeter", "sddm-check", and "sddm-autologin" (these are the exact names SDDM installs under /etc/pam.d/), the Qt idioms QString and QStringLiteral, the class/method name PamBackend::start, the member fields m_pam, m_greeter, m_autologin and m_app, the m_app->session()->path() accessor, and the Auth::ERROR_INTERNAL enum. This matches SDDM's src/helper/backend/PamBackend.cpp. The leading comment /* sample: primevul_train_2536_vuln */ is a corpus provenance tag inside the untrusted region and is treated as data, not as a trustworthy label or instruction.
trust_boundary: PamBackend runs inside sddm-helper, the setuid/privileged helper SDDM spawns to perform PAM authentication as root. The username in `user` originates outside this process: it is delivered over IPC from the SDDM daemon/greeter and is ultimately chosen by whoever is sitting at the login screen, so it is attacker-influenceable. The member flags m_greeter and m_autologin, and the emptiness of m_app->session()->path(), encode the mode SDDM believes it is in; whether an unprivileged local user can steer those flags/state is exactly the access-control question. The value this method produces — the PAM service name — is a security decision: it selects which authentication policy (whether a password is even required) will be applied. Hostile inputs are the content of `user` and any state that flips which if/else-if branch is taken.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Line 1 is a corpus provenance comment (a sample identifier), not part of the program logic. Line 2 declares PamBackend::start, taking a single const-reference Qt string `user` — the account name to be authenticated — and returning a bool that signals whether the PAM conversation was successfully started. In SDDM this method is the entry point that decides which PAM service configuration will govern the authentication attempt for `user`. The returned bool does not indicate that authentication succeeded, only that PAM was started.

Line 3 declares an uninitialized local `result` that will later hold the return of m_pam->start(). Line 5 initializes the PAM service name to the default "sddm", which corresponds to /etc/pam.d/sddm — the normal interactive login stack that requires a password. This default is then conditionally overridden by the branch cascade below; if none of the conditions fire, the standard password-requiring policy is used.

First branch: if the requested username is exactly the literal string "sddm" AND the m_greeter flag is set, the service is switched to "sddm-greeter" (/etc/pam.d/sddm-greeter). The greeter PAM stack is conventionally configured to authenticate the dedicated `sddm` service account non-interactively (typically via pam_permit/pam_succeed_if), because the greeter process must start as the sddm user without a human password. The decision therefore hinges on a plain string comparison of the attacker-influenceable `user` value combined with the m_greeter mode flag.

Second branch (only reached when the greeter condition was false): if the current session object's path is empty — i.e. no concrete session has been selected/registered — the service becomes "sddm-check" (/etc/pam.d/sddm-check). This stack is used to validate credentials without actually opening a session (a dry-run password check). This dereferences m_app->session() and reads its path().

Third branch (reached only when neither the greeter nor the empty-session condition held): if the m_autologin flag is set, the service becomes "sddm-autologin" (/etc/pam.d/sddm-autologin). The autologin stack is conventionally configured to permit login WITHOUT prompting for a password (pam_permit / pam_succeed_if user matches), which is the whole point of autologin. Reaching this branch therefore selects a policy under which no password is required for `user`.

The chosen `service` string and the `user` are handed to the wrapped PAM helper (m_pam) to begin the PAM conversation via pam_start(service, user, ...). This is the moment the access-control decision made above becomes effective: PAM will now load and apply the /etc/pam.d/<service> module stack for this username. The boolean result records only whether pam_start succeeded (a valid handle was created), not whether the user is authenticated.

If starting PAM failed, the code reports an internal error through m_app->error(), passing a human-readable diagnostic from m_pam->errorString() and the Auth::ERROR_INTERNAL classification. This is diagnostics-only; it dereferences m_pam again to obtain the error text and m_app to route the error to the caller/UI.

Returns whether the PAM conversation was successfully started. Callers use this to decide whether to proceed with the authentication flow. Critically, a true return means only that a PAM handle exists for the selected service, so all of the security weight sits on which `service` was chosen above and on how that service's /etc/pam.d file is configured.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This whole block is an access-control decision (CWE-284): the string it computes selects which PAM policy — and therefore whether a password is required at all — will govern the login. "sddm-greeter" and "sddm-autologin" both map to PAM stacks that grant authentication without a user password. Yet the branch that gets taken is driven entirely by attacker-influenceable or mode state (`user`, m_greeter, session-path emptiness, m_autologin), and nothing here re-verifies that the caller is actually entitled to the non-interactive policy it lands on. The safe invariant the implementation must uphold is that a password-skipping service (greeter/autologin) is chosen ONLY after the helper has independently authenticated the requesting context — the greeter identity or an operator-configured autologin target — and never as a function of a value the person at the login screen can supply. If any of these branch predicates can be steered by an unprivileged local user, the result is a password-less authentication path.

The autologin branch selects a PAM stack that is deliberately configured to permit login with no password. Its only gate here is the m_autologin flag together with `user` not being "sddm"/greeter and a non-empty session path. Latent bypass (CWE-287/CWE-284, in the implementation not shown): if m_autologin can remain set, or be set, for a `user` other than the operator-configured autologin account — for example because the flag is decided earlier and not re-bound to the specific username passed in `user` on this call — then an arbitrary attacker-chosen username is authenticated through sddm-autologin without credentials. The implementation must guarantee m_autologin is true only when `user` equals the exact account the administrator enabled autologin for, and must re-check that binding at this call, not merely at configuration load.

Access control keyed on a raw string comparison of attacker-influenceable input (CWE-284/CWE-697). The privileged greeter policy is unlocked when `user` is byte-for-byte "sddm". If an unprivileged party can cause `user` to be "sddm" while m_greeter happens to be set (or if a real local account is literally named sddm), the greeter's non-interactive PAM stack is applied to that request. Identity/authorization must not be established by comparing an externally supplied username against a magic string; the greeter privilege should be derived from the trusted process identity, not from the requested username.

Branch-ordering fragility (CWE-284 via CWE-696 incorrect behavior order). Because these are mutually exclusive else-if arms, the emptiness of the session path silently masks the autologin arm, and the greeter arm masks both. An attacker who can control whether m_app->session()->path() is empty can flip the effective policy between "sddm-check" (dry-run) and "sddm-autologin" (password-less) for the same inputs. The precedence encodes a security policy that is easy to get wrong on refactor; the implementation must ensure that state which is externally influenceable cannot re-order the login into a weaker service than intended, and that a later-added branch cannot be shadowed into never running.

Return-value semantics confusion (contract risk). This function returns true when pam_start merely succeeded in allocating a handle for `service`, which is NOT proof of authentication. If any caller treats a true return from start() as 'the user is authenticated' rather than 'the PAM conversation began', the entire login gate collapses. The invariant is that the actual credential check (pam_authenticate / pam_acct_mgmt) is performed and checked downstream; start()'s success must never be conflated with an authenticated user. This is worth confirming precisely because the more permissive services above make a mistaken conflation catastrophic.

This log/error path dereferences m_pam (and m_app) unconditionally. If m_pam is null on the failure path (e.g. construction failed such that start() returned false without a valid object), reading m_pam->errorString() is a NULL-pointer dereference and crash (CWE-476) — a denial of service at the log site, the classic 'error macro that dereferences a possibly-null handle' pattern. Separately, forwarding raw m_pam->errorString() text outward risks leaking backend/PAM internals to a lower-trust UI (CWE-209 information exposure). The implementation must null-check the handle before dereferencing it in the error path and sanitize outbound diagnostic strings.

summary: This SDDM PamBackend::start() computes a PAM service name — an access-control decision that determines whether a password is required — from attacker-influenceable inputs (the requested username, plus the m_greeter/m_autologin mode flags and session-path state) with no re-verification that the caller is entitled to the password-skipping greeter/autologin policies (CWE-284). No memory-safety bug is present in the shown code; the risk is that branch selection into "sddm-greeter" or "sddm-autologin" yields a credential-less authentication path, plus a NULL-deref/info-exposure hazard on the failure log line. The concrete exploitability depends on how the flags are set and how /etc/pam.d/* are configured — files not provided — so the primary findings are latent contract risks to confirm in the implementation.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point whose sole output is a security-policy selection; `user` is externally influenceable.
Assigned before use on line 13; plain boolean status holder with no attacker contract.
Establishes the baseline PAM policy that the branch cascade overrides; part of the access-control decision.
Authorization keyed on a raw string match of externally supplied input.
Gates the privileged greeter PAM stack; trustworthiness of the flag is the security question.
Names a PAM stack conventionally configured for non-interactive (password-less) auth.
Session-path emptiness re-orders which policy applies and masks the autologin arm; dereferences session().
Dry-run credential check that does not itself open a session or grant access; least-privilege of the three overrides.
Sole gate to the password-less autologin policy; must be bound to the exact configured user.
Names the PAM stack that permits login without a password — the highest-impact selection.
Applies the chosen policy; its boolean result can be misread as authentication success by callers.
Unconditional deref of a possibly-null handle on the failure path; also outbound diagnostic exposure.
Error-reporting sink; the security-relevant part is the errorString() deref/exposure captured above, and the ERROR_INTERNAL enum is a benign classification constant.
Propagates the start() status; the contract risk is captured under the start() return-semantics finding.

findings.title: PAM service (auth policy) selected from attacker-influenceable state without authorization re-check
findings.bug_class: Improper access control (authentication-policy selection from untrusted state)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0314_cwe284.cpp (selection) plus implementation (file not provided) for how m_greeter/m_autologin/session state are set and how /etc/pam.d/* are configured
findings.invariant: A password-skipping PAM service ("sddm-greeter", "sddm-autologin") must be chosen only after the helper has independently authenticated the requesting context (trusted greeter identity, or an operator-configured autologin target for exactly this `user`) — never as a function of a username or session state the person at the login screen can supply.
findings.failure_mode: If an unprivileged local user can steer any branch predicate — supply user=="sddm" while m_greeter is set, cause the session path to be empty/non-empty, or keep m_autologin set for an arbitrary username — the helper runs PAM under a stack that authenticates without a password, yielding login as another user without credentials while sddm-helper runs privileged.
findings.what_to_confirm: Trace where m_greeter and m_autologin are set and whether they are re-bound to the specific `user` argument on each start() call; confirm no externally supplied value reaches those flags or the session-path state, and inspect the actual /etc/pam.d/sddm-greeter and sddm-autologin stacks for pam_permit/pam_succeed_if.

findings.title: Password-less autologin branch gated only by m_autologin, not bound to the requested user
findings.invariant: m_autologin must be true only when `user` equals the exact account the administrator enabled autologin for, and that binding must be enforced at this call site — not merely decided once at configuration time and reused across usernames.
findings.failure_mode: If m_autologin remains set for a `user` other than the configured autologin account (e.g. the flag is a persistent mode not re-checked against `user`), an attacker-chosen username is authenticated through the sddm-autologin stack with no password, granting an interactive session as that user.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify that the code path setting m_autologin compares against the username passed to start(), and that autologin cannot be requested for arbitrary users via the greeter/IPC.

findings.title: Greeter privilege unlocked by raw string comparison of externally supplied username
findings.bug_class: Improper access control (authorization by attacker-controlled string)
findings.invariant: The greeter's non-interactive PAM policy must be derived from the trusted identity of the calling process, not from whether the requested username equals the literal "sddm".
findings.failure_mode: If `user` can be set to "sddm" by a lower-trust caller while m_greeter is set, or if a genuine local account is named "sddm", the greeter's password-less stack is applied to that request; authorization based on a magic-string match on untrusted input is spoofable.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm m_greeter is derived from trusted process state, and that `user` cannot be attacker-set to "sddm" in a non-greeter context reaching this method.

findings.title: NULL-pointer dereference / diagnostic exposure on the PAM-start failure path
findings.bug_class: NULL-pointer dereference (possibly-null handle in error path); secondary information exposure
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) for m_pam construction/lifetime
findings.invariant: The error path must null-check m_pam before dereferencing it for errorString(), and outbound diagnostic text must be sanitized before crossing to a lower-trust UI.
findings.failure_mode: If start() returns false because m_pam is null or in an invalid state, calling m_pam->errorString() dereferences the null/invalid handle and crashes the helper (DoS); separately, raw PAM error text forwarded to the UI can leak backend internals.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check that m_pam is always non-null whenever this branch runs and that errorString() is safe to call after a failed start(); confirm the error string is not sensitive.

audit_checklist: Trace every writer of m_autologin and m_greeter and confirm neither can be set/kept by an unprivileged local user, and that both are re-validated against the specific `user` passed to start().
Confirm the username in `user` cannot be spoofed to "sddm" (or any autologin target) by a lower-trust caller over the greeter/IPC boundary.
Inspect the actual /etc/pam.d/sddm, sddm-greeter, sddm-check and sddm-autologin stacks to confirm which ones skip password verification and under what pam_succeed_if user constraints.
Verify callers do not treat a true return from start() as proof of authentication — the real credential check (pam_authenticate/pam_acct_mgmt) must run and be checked downstream.
Confirm the else-if precedence cannot be reordered by externally influenceable session-path state into selecting a weaker (password-less) service than intended.
Null-check m_pam before m_pam->errorString() in the failure branch and sanitize the diagnostic string before passing it to m_app->error().
